summarization.a: Initial crystalline Si sample (27.17 × 27.17 × 38.04 Å³) before any ion bombardment.
summarization.b: Si sample after 10 eV CF₂⁺ bombardment. A thin (~4–5 Å) fluorocarbon layer forms on top with minimal disturbance to the underlying lattice.
summarization.c: Si sample after 20 eV CF₂⁺ bombardment. The passivation layer is thicker (~8 Å) and F/C atoms begin penetrating the near-surface Si.
summarization.d: Si sample after 30 eV CF₂⁺ bombardment. The fluorocarbon layer is thickest but concurrent Si etching occurs, and the surface appears shorter than the initial sample.

d.question: Why is 30 eV considered too high for passivation layer growth on Si?
d.answer: Because Si etching occurs simultaneously with deposition, preventing stable passivation layer formation.

a.question: How does the fluorocarbon layer change with increasing CF₂⁺ energy?
a.answer: The passivation layer thickens with energy: ~4–5 Å at 10 eV, ~8 Å at 20 eV, and broader still at 30 eV. However, higher energies also cause greater penetration of F and C into the Si lattice and increased amorphization. At 30 eV, concurrent Si etching begins to occur.

a.question: What atoms are shown in this figure?
What is the recommended CF₂⁺ energy range for passivating Si without etching?
